output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 1.0, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: Revised the draft to remove a flat self-contradiction (the intro claims end-to-end joint training while a later paragraph describes sequential per-component training; the closing paragraph also claims "supervision-free" while the methods section explicitly fine-tunes on gold AmbigNQ labels), tighten the contribution framing, and clean up prose. Below I first list the substantive problems, then give a revised draft, then flag what still needs author input.

---

Key problems in the current draft

1. Contradiction on training regime. Paragraph 1 says the system "trains a single seq2seq backbone that performs all three steps in one forward pass." Paragraph 2 says "The three components are trained sequentially, each with its own objective, and combined at inference time." These cannot both be true. The architecture description (separate retriever, classifier head, T5-large rewriter, and a separate FiD reader) is, in fact, a pipeline — exactly what paragraph 1 claims to avoid. Either the contribution claim or the method description must be rewritten. I assume below that the method description is accurate and the framing should be corrected; if the system really is end-to-end, the architecture paragraph needs to be rewritten instead.

2. Contradiction on supervision. Paragraph 3 states the model is fine-tuned on AmbigNQ gold disambiguated (question, answer) pairs, with explicit per-component objectives derived from AmbigNQ labels. Paragraph 4 then attributes gains to a "supervision-free approach" using Wikipedia disambiguation pages as weak signal. This is incoherent as written. Pick one story.

3. Unsupported attribution of gains. Even ignoring (2), the claim that gains over SpanSeqGen come from better generalization to held-out ambiguity types is not supported by any evidence in the draft (no held-out-type breakdown, no ablation, no comparison on novel ambiguity categories). Either cite the experiment or drop the causal claim.

4. Novelty framing is overstated and underspecified. "The first end-to-end generative model for ambiguity-aware open-domain QA" — AmbigQA / SpanSeqGen (Min et al., 2020) is already a generative ambiguity-aware system. The delta versus SpanSeqGen, Refuel, and similar work should be stated explicitly rather than via a blanket "first" claim. Also: 5.3 F1_ans over SpanSeqGen on dev is the headline number — is dev the right comparison, and is SpanSeqGen the right SOTA baseline as of submission?

5. Citations missing for load-bearing components. DPR, T5, Fusion-in-Decoder, and NQ-Open are all named without citation. Only Min et al. 2020 is cited.

6. Minor prose / specificity issues. "DPR-style" is vague; "top-100 passages" appears without justification; "21M passages" needs a Wikipedia snapshot date; F1_BLEU is reported without defining it for readers who don't know AmbigNQ's metrics.

---

Revised draft (resolving contradictions in favor of the methods description; flag in brackets where the author must confirm)

Recent work on retrieval-augmented open-domain QA has largely framed the task as single-answer prediction, even though a substantial fraction of natural-language questions admit multiple valid interpretations (Min et al., 2020). We present DISAMBIG-FiD, a generative ambiguity-aware QA system that detects question ambiguity, enumerates plausible interpretations, and emits a disambiguated (question, answer) pair for each. Relative to SpanSeqGen (Min et al., 2020), which generates a single sequence containing all disambiguations, DISAMBIG-FiD factorizes the problem into a retrieval-grounded classifier, a question rewriter, and a Fusion-in-Decoder reader (Izacard and Grave, 2021), allowing each interpretation to be answered against its own retrieved evidence.

The pipeline proceeds as follows. A DPR retriever (Karpukhin et al., 2020) over a Wikipedia index of 21M passages [snapshot: DATE] returns the top-100 passages for the input question. An ambiguity classifier head, conditioned on the question and the retrieved set, predicts a binary ambiguity label. When the question is judged ambiguous, a T5-large rewriter (Raffel et al., 2020) emits a set of disambiguated questions, and a Fusion-in-Decoder reader then answers each rewritten question against a freshly retrieved passage set. The three trainable components are optimized separately, each with its own objective, and composed at inference time.

We fine-tune on the AmbigNQ training split (Min et al., 2020), which provides gold disambiguated (question, answer) pairs for ambiguous NQ-Open questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). The retriever is trained with the standard DPR contrastive loss; the classifier with binary cross-entropy on AmbigNQ ambiguity labels; the rewriter with teacher-forced cross-entropy on gold disambiguated questions; and the FiD reader with cross-entropy on gold answers.

On the AmbigNQ dev split, DISAMBIG-FiD achieves 48.1 F1_ans (all) and 41.7 F1_BLEU on disambiguated questions, an improvement of 5.3 F1_ans over SpanSeqGen. [Ablation results — e.g., contribution of per-interpretation retrieval versus single shared retrieval — go here; the current attribution of gains to generalization across ambiguity types needs either an experiment or removal.]

DISAMBIG-FiD is a step toward QA systems that surface, rather than collapse, the multiple legitimate interpretations of user queries. Code and checkpoints will be released.

---

What the author still needs to decide / verify

- Is the system truly end-to-end (single backbone, one forward pass) or a pipeline of three separately trained components? The original draft asserts both. I rewrote assuming pipeline; if it is end-to-end, the methods paragraph must be replaced and the joint objective described.
- Is training supervised on AmbigNQ gold, or weakly supervised on Wikipedia disambiguation pages? I removed the "supervision-free" claim entirely because it is inconsistent with the stated objectives. If weak supervision is in fact used (in addition to or instead of AmbigNQ), that section needs to be rewritten and the comparison to SpanSeqGen revisited (different supervision regime ≠ apples-to-apples).
- "First end-to-end generative model" — this claim should be dropped or sharply qualified given SpanSeqGen's existence.
- The 5.3 F1_ans gain should be presented against the strongest published baseline at submission time (e.g., Refuel, AmbigQA variants), not only SpanSeqGen, and ideally on test as well as dev.
- Define F1_BLEU on first use, or cite the AmbigNQ metric definition.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the exact citation keys (Izacard and Grave 2021; Karpukhin et al. 2020; Raffel et al. 2020; Kwiatkowski et al. 2019) against the paper's bibliography, nor confirmed that SpanSeqGen's reported dev F1_ans is 42.8 (implied by the 5.3 delta) — the author should cross-check both before submission.
